10.note: Those who were not my people I will call my people, and her who was not beloved I will call beloved. And in the very place where it was said to them, You are not my people, there they will be called sons of the living God — Paul quotes Hos 1:10 and 2:23 together in Romans 9 to argue that Gentile inclusion in the people of God was always announced in the Scriptures. What Hosea promised for unfaithful Israel (restoration of covenant identity despite rejection) Paul reads as the anticipation of Gentile reception into the covenant. The passage names the same people-making act with the same vocabulary.

23.note: Once you were not a people, but now you are the people of God; once you had not received mercy, but now you have received mercy — Peter applies Hosea's restoration-oracle (I will say to Not My People: you are my people; and she will say: You are my God) directly to Gentile believers. Both Romans 9 and 1 Peter 2 use Hosea 2:23 to describe the same event: the ingrafting of Gentiles into the covenant people through Christ, enacting the reversal of Lo-Ammi that Hosea announced.

2.note: He was raised on the third day in accordance with the Scriptures — Paul's claim that the resurrection happened according to the Scriptures almost certainly includes Hos 6:2 (on the third day he will raise us up, that we may live before him) as one of the texts. Hosea 6:2 is not a direct resurrection prophecy but a pattern of national restoration through death-and-raising; Paul reads Christ's resurrection as fulfilling and exceeding this pattern, with the third day as the scriptural signature of YHWH's restorative act.

6.note: Go and learn what this means: I desire mercy, and not sacrifice — Jesus quotes Hos 6:6 twice in Matthew (9:13 and 12:7) as his hermeneutical key for reading Torah. In both cases he is responding to Pharisaic criticism: in Matt 9 over table fellowship with sinners, in Matt 12 over Sabbath gleaning. The Hosea quotation is Jesus's interpretive principle: the internal reality (hesed = covenant love/faithfulness) takes precedence over the external form. Hosea's critique of empty cultic religion becomes Jesus's criterion for reading the entire Law.

For I desire steadfast love and not sacrifice, the knowledge of God rather than burnt offerings — Jesus quotes Hos 6:6 twice (Matt 9:13; 12:7) in defense of his association with sinners and his disciples' plucking grain on the Sabbath; the priority of covenant love over ritual is Jesus's critique of Pharisaic misapplication of the law

1.note: Out of Egypt I called my son — Matthew cites Hos 11:1 as fulfilled in the flight to Egypt and the return: as YHWH called Israel (his son) out of Egypt in the Exodus, so Jesus (the true Son) recapitulates Israel's history; Jesus is the new Israel who succeeds where Israel failed
